name: Nidorino
route: Kanto Safari Zone, Johto badlands, rocky plains, and male challenge grounds marked by gouges and venom-scented soil
habitat: marshes, scrub, waste sites, damp caves, rotting logs, and contaminated urban edges. Field anchors: Kanto Safari Zone, Johto badlands, rocky plains, and male challenge grounds marked by gouges and venom-scented soil
classification: Poison Pin Pokemon / Male Venom Subadult
behaviorTemperament: Nidorino is best treated as a stage one field organism with poison-type instincts. In the wild it prioritizes food, shelter, breeding security, and escape routes before accepting contact. Stress signs usually appear before attack, but handlers often miss them because the Pokemon's powers look theatrical rather than biological.
diet: small prey, carrion, insects, contaminated plant matter, and toxin-rich foods handled through specialized organs
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular, with local variation based on food, weather, and human pressure.
bestTimeToFind: Early morning or late afternoon near known tracks, feeding signs, shelter edges, or route crossings
observationDifficulty: Moderate to high. It may be obvious in ideal habitat, but safe identification requires distance and attention to warning signs.
populationStatus: Stable where habitat remains intact, but local pressure increases near roads, farms, mines, labs, and tourist routes.
description: A Poison Pokemon listed as Poison Pin Pokemon. This real-world field profile keeps its game powers intact while treating them as biological hazards shaped by habitat, stress, and survival needs.
lore: Before Nidorino was treated as a cataloged species, travelers in its range reported small but repeatable hazards: disturbed camps, strange tracks, injured livestock, missing food, and warning signs that matched no ordinary animal. Later surveys tied those reports to Kanto Safari Zone, Johto badlands, rocky plains, and male challenge grounds marked by gouges and venom-scented soil. The species is now handled as a Poison field organism whose powers are real but still shaped by habitat, food, breeding pressure, and fear. Old local accounts often exaggerate Nidorino into a monster. The safer interpretation is more practical: it is an animal with extraordinary biology, and most injuries happen when humans treat a warning display as curiosity instead of a boundary.
survivalUse: Can help a trained ranger locate habitat clues tied to poison activity, such as food sources, shelter, water, weather changes, or dangerous terrain. Usefulness ends quickly if the animal is frightened, hungry, nesting, injured, or evolving.
handlingWarning: Do not corner Nidorino, handle it casually, or force contact during feeding, nesting, evolution stress, or injury. Main hazards are venom, irritant fluid, and contaminated surfaces. Maintain retreat space and use trained handlers for capture or care.
encounterAdvice: Stay upwind of Nidorino, keep skin covered, avoid touching shed material, and withdraw if it displays venom, hooding, barbs, colored fluid, or warning posture.
firstAid: For suspected venom or toxin contact, remove contaminated clothing, rinse with clean water, keep the victim calm, mark swelling spread, and seek urgent medical care.
companionSuitability: Possible only for experienced handlers with species-specific housing
fieldBehaviors: Displays warning colors before venom use
Avoids direct sun when toxins dry out
fieldNote: A calm Nidorino usually gives space before contact. A sudden change in sound, posture, glow, scent, temperature, or eye focus should be treated as the final warning before a poison-type response.
originStory: The Nidoran Male Evolution Family appears to have adapted around Kanto Routes 3 and 22, Safari Zone scrub, Johto grasslands, rocky burrow fields, and thorned territorial paths. Its line favors survival through venom, irritant fluid, and contaminated surfaces, specialized shelter choice, and rapid behavioral changes during maturity. Nidoran♂ becomes Nidorino by Level 16; Nidorino becomes Nidoking by Moon Stone. In field terms, each stage is best read as a different ecological job: the youngest form survives by hiding, warning, or fleeing; intermediate forms defend territory and gather resources; final forms dominate the local niche or stabilize the colony. This dossier treats evolution as a real biological threshold triggered by growth, stress, energy reserves, and the listed game-derived method.

evolutionLine.name: Nidoran♂
evolutionLine.note: Base-stage Poison species. Growth, training, survival stress, and the listed method lead toward the next form.

evolutionLine.name: Nidorino
evolutionLine.note: Intermediate Poison stage. Body systems are reorganizing toward the final form and behavior may be unstable.

evolutionLine.name: Nidoking
evolutionLine.note: Final-stage Poison / Ground species within the first-151 field scope. Manage as a mature adult, not a juvenile.

mainLine.name: Nidoran♂

mainLine.name: Nidorino
mainLine.note: Intermediate Poison stage. Body systems are reorganizing toward the final form and behavior may be unstable.

mainLine.name: Nidoking
mainLine.note: Final-stage Poison / Ground species within the first-151 field scope. Manage as a mature adult, not a juvenile.

domesticationStatus: Mostly wild with licensed handling possible
prey: small prey, carrion, insects, contaminated plant matter, and toxin-rich foods handled through specialized organs
temperamentTriggers: Improper response to poison-type warning signs
territoryRange: 1 to 5 square miles depending on food, nesting sites, and competition
lifeCycle: Hatches or emerges as a juvenile adapted to marshes, feeds heavily, defends shelter, and follows this first-151 evolution profile: Nidoran♂ evolves into Nidorino by Level 16. Nidorino evolves into Nidoking by Moon Stone.
containmentRequirements: Requires negative-pressure space, washable flooring, and antidote planning, food control, shelter enrichment, double-door access, species-specific medical plan, and emergency standoff based on its listed attacks.
ecologicalRole: Fills a poison-type niche by regulating prey, plants, waste, terrain, weather pressure, or predator behavior in its native habitat. Removing it can cause local imbalance even when the species is hazardous.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Concerns include wound infection, parasites from native habitat, stress illness, and secondary exposure related to envenomation, swelling, numbness, contaminated clothing, and delayed systemic symptoms
reproductionNesting: Nests or shelters in protected parts of its habitat. Eggs or young are usually guarded more strongly during storms, drought, cold snaps, food shortage, or evolution season.
predators: Larger birds, snakes, aquatic predators, stronger territorial Pokemon, humans, disease, and habitat loss
emergencyResponse: Secure bystanders, stop pursuit, move to cover, and treat the scene as a poison hazard area. Do not attempt capture unless trained and equipped for Nidorino.
legalStatus: Permit recommended or required in settled areas, especially for transport, breeding, public display, or combat training
lifespan: Official lifespan is not stated. Field estimate: wild 2 to 6 years; managed care 10 to 20 years depending on species size and evolution stage.
socialStructure: dominant adults with guarded ranges or stable colonies

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Main hazard comes from body contact and species-specific attacks.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Maintain normal standoff based on speed, terrain, and behavior.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Field burst speed roughly 16 to 29 mph depending on terrain and age
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Variable by body plan; assume sudden lunges over short obstacles are possible
fieldCapabilities.balance: Strong in native habitat; reduced on slick artificial flooring or unfamiliar confinement

trainingRisk: Training risk includes envenomation, swelling, numbness, contaminated clothing, and delayed systemic symptoms. Evolution-stage changes can make a formerly safe individual unsafe without warning.
physiology: Nidorino's body supports Poison output through specialized organs, defensive tissues, and stress-responsive behavior. Its attacks are not magic tricks in this guide; they are treated as biological systems that consume energy, create waste heat or toxins, and fail when the animal is sick.

keyFacts.diet: small prey, carrion, insects, contaminated plant matter, and toxin-rich foods handled through specialized organs
keyFacts.habitat: marshes, scrub, waste sites, damp caves, rotting logs, and contaminated urban edges
keyFacts.lifespan: Official lifespan not stated; see lifespan field for field estimate.
keyFacts.groupSize: small groups, pairs, or local colonies depending on habitat

physicalCharacteristics: Recognizable as poison pin pokemon with poison adaptations, field coloration, specialized attack structures, and body proportions matching its official Pokédex profile.
dietRestrictions: Avoid human junk food, contaminated prey, unfamiliar supplements, forced fasting, and habitat-inappropriate foods. Diet changes should be gradual and matched to type biology.

attacks.name: Poison Sting
attacks.fieldUse: venom prick or spine jab used to test distance and discourage handling

attacks.name: Acid
attacks.fieldUse: caustic spray used against plants, cloth, and soft tissue

attacks.name: Toxic
attacks.fieldUse: persistent venom contamination used to make predators back away

attacks.name: Sludge Bomb
attacks.fieldUse: thick toxic projectile that contaminates soil and gear

Kanto: Kanto Safari Zone, Johto badlands, rocky plains, and male challenge grounds marked by gouges and venom-scented soil
JohtoAndLater: Johto, Hoenn, Sinnoh, Unova, Kalos, Alola, and later-region appearances vary by game version and habitat compatibility
realWorldAnalog: Use matching terrain analogs rather than real geography alone: old forests, caves, coasts, wetlands, mountains, power corridors, urban margins, or protected preserves depending on the species.

officialEvolutionSummary.summary: Nidoran♂ evolves into Nidorino by Level 16. Nidorino evolves into Nidoking by Moon Stone.

sourceUseNote: Core names, numbering, typing, height, weight, base stats, and first-151 evolution methods are based on public Pokedex and game database references. Lifespan, ecology, first aid, containment, and real-world behavior are interpretive survival-guide material for this passion project.
